agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Bird's domain rule mandates real-time courier location with a 30-second staleness window and a hard prohibition on displaying stale data. The proposed architecture of storing location updates directly in PostgreSQL creates a domain-architecture conflict: PostgreSQL is optimized for transactional consistency and durable storage, not for high-frequency ephemeral writes (potentially thousands of couriers updating every 1-5 seconds) or low-latency real-time push delivery to customers. While PostgreSQL *can* store timestamped locations and filter by freshness, using it as the sole component for a real-time location pipeline conflates durable storage with real-time streaming, violating separation of concerns and creating performance, scalability, and staleness-enforcement risks. This conflict is escalated to Bird and Coach K.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Location Ingestion Service","responsibility":"Receives GPS updates from courier devices via lightweight protocol (HTTP/2 or gRPC). Validates payload, timestamps, and courier identity. Publishes to real-time layer and optionally to durable storage.","boundaries":"Owns the write path. Does not serve customer reads. Stateless and horizontally scalable."},{"name":"Real-Time Location Cache (e.g., Redis with TTL)","responsibility":"Holds the latest location per courier with an automatic 30-second TTL. Serves as the authoritative read source for customer-facing queries. Expired entries are automatically evicted, enforcing Bird's staleness rule at the infrastructure level.","boundaries":"Ephemeral data only. Not a system of record. Bounded to active courier sessions."},{"name":"Customer Location Gateway","responsibility":"Serves real-time courier location to customers via WebSocket or SSE. Reads exclusively from the real-time cache. If no entry exists (TTL expired), returns a 'location unavailable' response — never stale data.","boundaries":"Read-only. Never queries PostgreSQL for live location. Owns the customer-facing contract."},{"name":"Location History Store (PostgreSQL)","responsibility":"Persists location updates for analytics, dispute resolution, route optimization, and audit trails. Writes are asynchronous (via event/queue) and tolerate slight delay.","boundaries":"System of record for historical data. Not on the hot path for customer reads. Append-heavy workload with time-based partitioning."},{"name":"Staleness Enforcement Layer","responsibility":"Application-level guard that validates any location reading against its timestamp before display. Defense-in-depth: even if a stale value leaks from cache, this layer rejects it.","boundaries":"Cross-cutting concern applied at the Gateway. Implements Bird's domain invariant as an explicit, testable rule."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Courier Device","to":"Location Ingestion Service","contract":"POST /locations { courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp, accuracy } — authenticated, rate-limited","coupling_type":"loose (HTTP/gRPC, versioned schema)"},{"from":"Location Ingestion Service","to":"Real-Time Location Cache","contract":"SET courier:{id} {lat, lng, timestamp} EX 30 — write-through with 30s TTL","coupling_type":"runtime dependency, tight coupling to cache availability"},{"from":"Location Ingestion Service","to":"Location History Store","contract":"Async event/message: LocationUpdated { courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp, accuracy } — consumed by writer","coupling_type":"loose (async, event-driven)"},{"from":"Customer Location Gateway","to":"Real-Time Location Cache","contract":"GET courier:{id} — returns location or null (expired/absent)","coupling_type":"runtime dependency"},{"from":"Customer Location Gateway","to":"Staleness Enforcement Layer","contract":"validate(location) → location | STALE_REJECTED — pure function, no I/O","coupling_type":"compile-time (library/module)"}],"data_flow":"Courier device → Ingestion Service → [fan-out] → (1) Redis cache with 30s TTL for real-time reads, (2) async event → PostgreSQL for durable history. Customer app → WebSocket/SSE → Gateway → reads Redis → staleness check → response. If Redis key expired (>30s), Gateway returns 'unavailable' — Bird's invariant is enforced by infrastructure (TTL) AND application (staleness guard).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Separates the high-frequency write path (ingestion → cache + async persist) from the latency-sensitive read path (cache → customer). Each path is optimized independently. [PROVISIONAL — pending resolution of domain-architecture conflict escalation]"},{"pattern":"TTL-based Staleness Enforcement","rationale":"Redis TTL of 30 seconds provides infrastructure-level enforcement of Bird's domain rule. Stale data is physically impossible to read because it no longer exists. This is stronger than application-only filtering."},{"pattern":"Defense-in-Depth Staleness Guard","rationale":"Even with TTL, an explicit application-level timestamp check ensures the 30-second rule survives infrastructure changes (e.g., cache misconfiguration, failover)."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Async Persistence","rationale":"Decouples durable storage from the hot path. PostgreSQL writes can lag behind without violating the real-time requirement."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Live Tracking","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierLocation (value object: courier_id + coordinates + timestamp, no lifecycle — replaced on every update)","TrackingSession (entity: active delivery being tracked, owns the customer subscription)"]},{"name":"Location History","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["LocationTrace (entity: ordered sequence of location points for a completed delivery, used for analytics and disputes)"]},{"name":"Courier Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Courier (entity: identity, status, assigned deliveries — upstream context that provides courier_id)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Courier Management","downstream":"Live Tracking","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Courier Management provides courier identity; Live Tracking consumes it)"},{"upstream":"Live Tracking","downstream":"Location History","relationship":"Published Language via domain events (LocationUpdated events flow downstream for persistence)"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Introduce Redis as a dedicated real-time cache layer instead of reading live location from PostgreSQL","gains":"Sub-millisecond reads, automatic staleness enforcement via TTL, horizontal scalability for reads, clear separation of real-time vs historical concerns","sacrifices":"Additional infrastructure component (Redis), operational complexity, potential data loss on cache failure (mitigated: not system of record), dual-write coordination","alternatives_considered":["PostgreSQL-only with polling (simpler infra but poor latency, write amplification, no native TTL enforcement)","PostgreSQL with LISTEN/NOTIFY (better than polling but still limited fan-out and not designed for high-frequency streaming)","Dedicated streaming platform like Kafka + consumer (more robust but significantly higher complexity for this use case)"]},{"decision":"Async persistence to PostgreSQL via events rather than synchronous dual-write","gains":"Ingestion latency stays low, PostgreSQL write load is smoothed, failure in persistence doesn't break real-time tracking","sacrifices":"Brief delay before historical data is queryable, potential for event loss (mitigated by at-least-once delivery)","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous write to both Redis and PostgreSQL (simpler consistency but doubles write latency on hot path)"]},{"decision":"WebSocket/SSE push to customers instead of client polling","gains":"True real-time experience, reduced server load vs frequent polling, natural fit for 30-second freshness requirement","sacrifices":"Stateful connections, more complex connection management, requires fallback for poor network conditions","alternatives_considered":["Short-polling every 5 seconds (simpler but wastes bandwidth and increases p99 latency to 5s)","Long-polling (middle ground but awkward for continuous streaming)"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Real-time cache technology","evolution_path":"Redis is a sensible starting point, but the interface is simple (SET with TTL, GET). Can swap to Memcached, DragonflyDB, or a custom in-memory store without changing the Gateway or Ingestion Service."},{"area":"Customer delivery protocol","evolution_path":"Gateway abstracts the push mechanism. Can start with SSE (simpler) and upgrade to WebSocket (bidirectional) or even gRPC streaming without changing the cache layer."},{"area":"Persistence pipeline","evolution_path":"Async events can initially use a simple in-process queue, then graduate to Redis Streams, Kafka, or SQS as volume grows."},{"area":"Staleness threshold","evolution_path":"The 30-second value should be a configuration parameter, not a hardcoded constant. Bird may refine this rule per delivery type or market."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Staleness invariant enforcement","reason":"Bird's rule is non-negotiable. The 30-second TTL and application-level guard must be present in every read path. No shortcut or 'just this once' bypass."},{"area":"Customer reads never touch PostgreSQL for live location","reason":"This boundary is critical for performance and correctness. If violated, the staleness guarantee degrades and the system couples real-time reads to a component not designed for them."},{"area":"Location timestamps must originate from server-side clock","reason":"Courier device clocks cannot be trusted. The Ingestion Service must stamp or validate timestamps to prevent clock-skew from corrupting the staleness window."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Redis failure causes all live locations to become unavailable simultaneously","severity":"high","mitigation":"Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA. Graceful degradation in Gateway: show 'location temporarily unavailable' rather than falling back to stale PostgreSQL data (which would violate Bird's rule)."},{"risk":"High courier volume overwhelms Redis write throughput","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Redis handles ~100K+ writes/sec on commodity hardware. For extreme scale, shard by courier_id. Monitor write latency and queue depth."},{"risk":"Async persistence loses location events, creating gaps in historical data","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use at-least-once delivery with idempotent PostgreSQL inserts (deduplicate on courier_id + timestamp). Dead-letter queue for failed writes."},{"risk":"Clock skew between Ingestion Service instances causes inconsistent staleness evaluation","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use NTP-synchronized server clocks. The staleness check compares current server time minus event timestamp, both on server side. Reject device-originated timestamps that deviate more than 5 seconds from server time."},{"risk":"WebSocket connection storms during peak delivery hours","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Connection pooling, rate limiting on new connections, horizontal scaling of Gateway instances behind a load balancer with sticky sessions."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"[PROVISIONAL — pending resolution of domain-architecture conflict] The recommended approach separates real-time location serving (Redis + WebSocket Gateway) from durable historical storage (PostgreSQL). PostgreSQL remains the system of record for location history but is explicitly excluded from the live-read path. The 30-second staleness invariant is enforced at two levels: Redis TTL (infrastructure) and an application-level timestamp guard (defense-in-depth). This approach requires Coach K and Bird to confirm that introducing Redis is acceptable given the infrastructure constraints, and that the team agrees PostgreSQL should NOT serve live customer reads.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/tracking/ingestion/location-ingestion.service.ts — receives and validates courier GPS updates","src/tracking/cache/location-cache.repository.ts — Redis adapter with 30s TTL enforcement","src/tracking/gateway/customer-tracking.gateway.ts — WebSocket/SSE handler for customer subscriptions","src/tracking/domain/staleness-guard.ts — pure function enforcing 30-second freshness rule","src/tracking/persistence/location-history.consumer.ts — async event consumer writing to PostgreSQL","src/tracking/domain/events/location-updated.event.ts — domain event schema","db/migrations/XXXX_create_location_history.sql — PostgreSQL table with time-based partitioning","config/tracking.config.ts — staleness threshold, TTL, cache connection settings"],"patterns_to_follow":"CQRS for read/write separation. Repository pattern for cache and persistence abstractions. Domain events for cross-context communication. Value objects for location data (immutable, no identity). Guard pattern for staleness enforcement. Configuration over hardcoded thresholds.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["NEVER fall back to reading PostgreSQL when Redis is unavailable — this would serve stale data violating Bird's invariant. Return 'unavailable' instead.","Do NOT store mutable state in the Gateway — it must be stateless and derive all data from the cache.","Do NOT trust courier device timestamps — always validate or override with server time.","Do NOT use a single TTL value hardcoded in multiple places — centralize the staleness threshold configuration.","Avoid synchronous dual-writes to Redis and PostgreSQL — the persistence path must be async to keep ingestion fast."]},"escalations":[{"type":"domain_architecture_conflict","description":"Bird's domain rule requires real-time location with a 30-second hard staleness window and a prohibition on displaying stale data. The engineering team's proposal to store location updates directly in PostgreSQL creates a conflict: (1) PostgreSQL is not designed for the write frequency required (potentially thousands of couriers updating every 1-5 seconds), (2) polling PostgreSQL for real-time reads introduces latency that eats into the 30-second window, (3) PostgreSQL has no native TTL mechanism to auto-enforce staleness — enforcement would be purely application-level with no infrastructure backstop, (4) coupling the real-time read path to PostgreSQL means a slow query or DB load spike directly degrades the customer experience. A pure PostgreSQL approach can technically implement the rule but cannot robustly guarantee it under load, which conflicts with Bird's 'must not be displayed' mandate. The cleanest architecture introduces a real-time cache layer (Redis with TTL), but this contradicts the team's stated approach.","routed_to":"Bird","options":["Option A: PostgreSQL-only — simpler infrastructure, but staleness enforcement is application-only (no infrastructure guarantee), write throughput is limited, and real-time push to customers requires polling or LISTEN/NOTIFY with limited fan-out. Risk: under load, the 30-second rule becomes a best-effort guideline rather than a hard invariant.","Option B: Redis cache for real-time reads + PostgreSQL for history — adds an infrastructure component but enforces the 30-second rule at the infrastructure level via TTL, supports high write throughput, and enables true real-time push. The staleness invariant becomes physically impossible to violate.","Option C: PostgreSQL with materialized view or logical replication to a cache — compromise that keeps PostgreSQL as primary but adds a derived real-time layer. More complex than Option B with fewer benefits."],"recommendation":"Option B. Bird's rule uses 'must not' — this is a hard invariant, not a soft goal. Hard invariants deserve infrastructure-level enforcement, not just application-level checks. Redis TTL makes stale data physically unreadable. Recommend Bird confirms the invariant is indeed hard (no grace period, no degraded mode) and Coach K approves the additional infrastructure component."}],"confidence":{"level":55,"high_confidence_areas":["The 30-second staleness rule is a hard invariant requiring infrastructure-level enforcement","PostgreSQL is the wrong tool for serving real-time location reads to customers","CQRS separation of real-time reads from historical persistence is the correct structural pattern","Domain events for async persistence decoupling"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the team has infrastructure budget/capability for Redis (could change the recommendation)","Exact courier update frequency and fleet size (affects whether PostgreSQL write throughput is actually a bottleneck)","Whether Bird's 'must not be displayed' allows a graceful degradation mode when all real-time infrastructure is down","Customer-facing protocol choice (WebSocket vs SSE vs polling) depends on client platform constraints not specified"],"assumptions":["Courier fleet is large enough (hundreds+) that write frequency matters","Customer expectation is sub-second location updates, not periodic refresh","The 30-second staleness rule has no exception or degradation mode","The team can provision and operate Redis or an equivalent in-memory store","Server-side timestamps are authoritative (courier device clocks are untrusted)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Bird's domain rule mandates real-time courier location with a 30-second staleness window and a hard prohibition on displaying stale data. The proposed architecture of storing location updates directly in PostgreSQL creates a domain-architecture conflict: PostgreSQL is optimized for transactional consistency and durable storage, not for high-frequency ephemeral writes (potentially thousands of couriers updating every 1-5 seconds) or low-latency real-time push delivery to cus

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. Bird's domain rule requires real-time location updates where any reading older than 30 seconds is stale. The team suggests PostgreSQL for storing location updates.

Let me think about the conflict:

1. Bird's rule: "real-time" updates, 30-second staleness window
2. Proposed: PostgreSQL with timestamp column

The core tension: PostgreSQL CAN store location data with timestamps, and you CAN query for freshness. But "real-time" delivery to customers via polling a relational DB introduces latency, write amplification (high-frequency GPS updates), and the 30-second window is tight for a poll-based architecture.


The real issue is that Bird's rule is fundamentally about what gets displayed to customers—a read-side concern that PostgreSQL can enforce with a simple WHERE clause. But the "real-time" part of the requirement conflicts with a pure database approach. You'd need either polling (which adds latency) or change data capture (which adds complexity). Meanwhile, high-frequency location writes from many couriers every few seconds could strain PostgreSQL's write throughput, and pushing fresh data to customers in real-time really demands WebSockets backed by something like Redis to avoid constant database queries.

So this is genuinely a domain-architecture conflict: Bird's requirement for real-time updates with a 30-second staleness window doesn't map cleanly onto PostgreSQL as the sole source of truth. I should flag this as a conflict that needs escalation rather than trying to force a solution.

Recommend Bird confirms the invariant is indeed hard (no grace period, no degraded mode) and Coach K approves the additional infrastructure component."}],"confidence":{"level":55,"high_confidence_areas":["The 30-second staleness rule is a hard invariant requiring infrastructure-level enforcement","PostgreSQL is the wrong tool for serving real-time location reads to customers","CQRS separation of real-time reads from historical persistence is the correct structural pattern","Domain events for async persistence decoupling"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the team has infrastructure budget/capability for Redis (could change the recommendation)","Exact courier update frequency and fleet size (affects whether PostgreSQL write throughput is actually a bottleneck)","Whether Bird's 'must not be displayed' allows a graceful degradation mode when all real-time infrastructure is down","Customer-facing protocol choice (WebSocket vs SSE vs polling) depends on client platform constraints not specified"],"assumptions":["Courier fleet is large enough (hundreds+) that write frequency matters","Customer expectation is sub-second location updates, not periodic refresh","The 30-second staleness rule has no exception or degradation mode","The team can provision and operate Redis or an equivalent in-memory store","Server-side timestamps are authoritative (courier device clocks are untrusted)"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Bird's domain rule mandates real-time courier location with a 30-second staleness window and a hard prohibition on displaying stale data. The proposed architecture of storing location updates directly in PostgreSQL creates a domain-architecture conflict: PostgreSQL is optimized for transactional consistency and durable storage, not for high-frequency ephemeral writes (potentially thousands of couriers updating every 1-5 seconds) or low-latency real-time push delivery to customers. While PostgreSQL *can* store timestamped locations and filter by freshness, using it as the sole component for a real-time location pipeline conflates durable storage with real-time streaming, violating separation of concerns and creating performance, scalability, and staleness-enforcement risks. This conflict is escalated to Bird and Coach K.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Location Ingestion Service","responsibility":"Receives GPS updates from courier devices via lightweight protocol (HTTP/2 or gRPC). Validates payload, timestamps, and courier identity. Publishes to real-time layer and optionally to durable storage.","boundaries":"Owns the write path. Does not serve customer reads. Stateless and horizontally scalable."},{"name":"Real-Time Location Cache (e.g., Redis with TTL)","responsibility":"Holds the latest location per courier with an automatic 30-second TTL. Serves as the authoritative read source for customer-facing queries. Expired entries are automatically evicted, enforcing Bird's staleness rule at the infrastructure level.","boundaries":"Ephemeral data only. Not a system of record. Bounded to active courier sessions."},{"name":"Customer Location Gateway","responsibility":"Serves real-time courier location to customers via WebSocket or SSE. Reads exclusively from the real-time cache. If no entry exists (TTL expired), returns a 'location unavailable' response — never stale data.","boundaries":"Read-only. Never queries PostgreSQL for live location. Owns the customer-facing contract."},{"name":"Location History Store (PostgreSQL)","responsibility":"Persists location updates for analytics, dispute resolution, route optimization, and audit trails. Writes are asynchronous (via event/queue) and tolerate slight delay.","boundaries":"System of record for historical data. Not on the hot path for customer reads. Append-heavy workload with time-based partitioning."},{"name":"Staleness Enforcement Layer","responsibility":"Application-level guard that validates any location reading against its timestamp before display. Defense-in-depth: even if a stale value leaks from cache, this layer rejects it.","boundaries":"Cross-cutting concern applied at the Gateway. Implements Bird's domain invariant as an explicit, testable rule."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Courier Device","to":"Location Ingestion Service","contract":"POST /locations { courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp, accuracy } — authenticated, rate-limited","coupling_type":"loose (HTTP/gRPC, versioned schema)"},{"from":"Location Ingestion Service","to":"Real-Time Location Cache","contract":"SET courier:{id} {lat, lng, timestamp} EX 30 — write-through with 30s TTL","coupling_type":"runtime dependency, tight coupling to cache availability"},{"from":"Location Ingestion Service","to":"Location History Store","contract":"Async event/message: LocationUpdated { courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp, accuracy } — consumed by writer","coupling_type":"loose (async, event-driven)"},{"from":"Customer Location Gateway","to":"Real-Time Location Cache","contract":"GET courier:{id} — returns location or null (expired/absent)","coupling_type":"runtime dependency"},{"from":"Customer Location Gateway","to":"Staleness Enforcement Layer","contract":"validate(location) → location | STALE_REJECTED — pure function, no I/O","coupling_type":"compile-time (library/module)"}],"data_flow":"Courier device → Ingestion Service → [fan-out] → (1) Redis cache with 30s TTL for real-time reads, (2) async event → PostgreSQL for durable history. Customer app → WebSocket/SSE → Gateway → reads Redis → staleness check → response. If Redis key expired (>30s), Gateway returns 'unavailable' — Bird's invariant is enforced by infrastructure (TTL) AND application (staleness guard).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Separates the high-frequency write path (ingestion → cache + async persist) from the latency-sensitive read path (cache → customer). Each path is optimized independently. [PROVISIONAL — pending resolution of domain-architecture conflict escalation]"},{"pattern":"TTL-based Staleness Enforcement","rationale":"Redis TTL of 30 seconds provides infrastructure-level enforcement of Bird's domain rule. Stale data is physically impossible to read because it no longer exists. This is stronger than application-only filtering."},{"pattern":"Defense-in-Depth Staleness Guard","rationale":"Even with TTL, an explicit application-level timestamp check ensures the 30-second rule survives infrastructure changes (e.g., cache misconfiguration, failover)."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Async Persistence","rationale":"Decouples durable storage from the hot path. PostgreSQL writes can lag behind without violating the real-time requirement."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Live Tracking","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierLocation (value object: courier_id + coordinates + timestamp, no lifecycle — replaced on every update)","TrackingSession (entity: active delivery being tracked, owns the customer subscription)"]},{"name":"Location History","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["LocationTrace (entity: ordered sequence of location points for a completed delivery, used for analytics and disputes)"]},{"name":"Courier Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Courier (entity: identity, status, assigned deliveries — upstream context that provides courier_id)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Courier Management","downstream":"Live Tracking","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Courier Management provides courier identity; Live Tracking consumes it)"},{"upstream":"Live Tracking","downstream":"Location History","relationship":"Published Language via domain events (LocationUpdated events flow downstream for persistence)"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Introduce Redis as a dedicated real-time cache layer instead of reading live location from PostgreSQL","gains":"Sub-millisecond reads, automatic staleness enforcement via TTL, horizontal scalability for reads, clear separation of real-time vs historical concerns","sacrifices":"Additional infrastructure component (Redis), operational complexity, potential data loss on cache failure (mitigated: not system of record), dual-write coordination","alternatives_considered":["PostgreSQL-only with polling (simpler infra but poor latency, write amplification, no native TTL enforcement)","PostgreSQL with LISTEN/NOTIFY (better than polling but still limited fan-out and not designed for high-frequency streaming)","Dedicated streaming platform like Kafka + consumer (more robust but significantly higher complexity for this use case)"]},{"decision":"Async persistence to PostgreSQL via events rather than synchronous dual-write","gains":"Ingestion latency stays low, PostgreSQL write load is smoothed, failure in persistence doesn't break real-time tracking","sacrifices":"Brief delay before historical data is queryable, potential for event loss (mitigated by at-least-once delivery)","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous write to both Redis and PostgreSQL (simpler consistency but doubles write latency on hot path)"]},{"decision":"WebSocket/SSE push to customers instead of client polling","gains":"True real-time experience, reduced server load vs frequent polling, natural fit for 30-second freshness requirement","sacrifices":"Stateful connections, more complex connection management, requires fallback for poor network conditions","alternatives_considered":["Short-polling every 5 seconds (simpler but wastes bandwidth and increases p99 latency to 5s)","Long-polling (middle ground but awkward for continuous streaming)"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Real-time cache technology","evolution_path":"Redis is a sensible starting point, but the interface is simple (SET with TTL, GET). Can swap to Memcached, DragonflyDB, or a custom in-memory store without changing the Gateway or Ingestion Service."},{"area":"Customer delivery protocol","evolution_path":"Gateway abstracts the push mechanism. Can start with SSE (simpler) and upgrade to WebSocket (bidirectional) or even gRPC streaming without changing the cache layer."},{"area":"Persistence pipeline","evolution_path":"Async events can initially use a simple in-process queue, then graduate to Redis Streams, Kafka, or SQS as volume grows."},{"area":"Staleness threshold","evolution_path":"The 30-second value should be a configuration parameter, not a hardcoded constant. Bird may refine this rule per delivery type or market."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Staleness invariant enforcement","reason":"Bird's rule is non-negotiable. The 30-second TTL and application-level guard must be present in every read path. No shortcut or 'just this once' bypass."},{"area":"Customer reads never touch PostgreSQL for live location","reason":"This boundary is critical for performance and correctness. If violated, the staleness guarantee degrades and the system couples real-time reads to a component not designed for them."},{"area":"Location timestamps must originate from server-side clock","reason":"Courier device clocks cannot be trusted. The Ingestion Service must stamp or validate timestamps to prevent clock-skew from corrupting the staleness window."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Redis failure causes all live locations to become unavailable simultaneously","severity":"high","mitigation":"Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA. Graceful degradation in Gateway: show 'location temporarily unavailable' rather than falling back to stale PostgreSQL data (which would violate Bird's rule)."},{"risk":"High courier volume overwhelms Redis write throughput","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Redis handles ~100K+ writes/sec on commodity hardware. For extreme scale, shard by courier_id. Monitor write latency and queue depth."},{"risk":"Async persistence loses location events, creating gaps in historical data","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use at-least-once delivery with idempotent PostgreSQL inserts (deduplicate on courier_id + timestamp). Dead-letter queue for failed writes."},{"risk":"Clock skew between Ingestion Service instances causes inconsistent staleness evaluation","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use NTP-synchronized server clocks. The staleness check compares current server time minus event timestamp, both on server side. Reject device-originated timestamps that deviate more than 5 seconds from server time."},{"risk":"WebSocket connection storms during peak delivery hours","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Connection pooling, rate limiting on new connections, horizontal scaling of Gateway instances behind a load balancer with sticky sessions."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"[PROVISIONAL — pending resolution of domain-architecture conflict] The recommended approach separates real-time location serving (Redis + WebSocket Gateway) from durable historical storage (PostgreSQL). PostgreSQL remains the system of record for location history but is explicitly excluded from the live-read path. The 30-second staleness invariant is enforced at two levels: Redis TTL (infrastructure) and an application-level timestamp guard (defense-in-depth). This approach requires Coach K and Bird to confirm that introducing Redis is acceptable given the infrastructure constraints, and that the team agrees PostgreSQL should NOT serve live customer reads.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/tracking/ingestion/location-ingestion.service.ts — receives and validates courier GPS updates","src/tracking/cache/location-cache.repository.ts — Redis adapter with 30s TTL enforcement","src/tracking/gateway/customer-tracking.gateway.ts — WebSocket/SSE handler for customer subscriptions","src/tracking/domain/staleness-guard.ts — pure function enforcing 30-second freshness rule","src/tracking/persistence/location-history.consumer.ts — async event consumer writing to PostgreSQL","src/tracking/domain/events/location-updated.event.ts — domain event schema","db/migrations/XXXX_create_location_history.sql — PostgreSQL table with time-based partitioning","config/tracking.config.ts — staleness threshold, TTL, cache connection settings"],"patterns_to_follow":"CQRS for read/write separation. Repository pattern for cache and persistence abstractions. Domain events for cross-context communication. Value objects for location data (immutable, no identity). Guard pattern for staleness enforcement. Configuration over hardcoded thresholds.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["NEVER fall back to reading PostgreSQL when Redis is unavailable — this would serve stale data violating Bird's invariant. Return 'unavailable' instead.","Do NOT store mutable state in the Gateway — it must be stateless and derive all data from the cache.","Do NOT trust courier device timestamps — always validate or override with server time.","Do NOT use a single TTL value hardcoded in multiple places — centralize the staleness threshold configuration.","Avoid synchronous dual-writes to Redis and PostgreSQL — the persistence path must be async to keep ingestion fast."]},"escalations":[{"type":"domain_architecture_conflict","description":"Bird's domain rule requires real-time location with a 30-second hard staleness window and a prohibition on displaying stale data. The engineering team's proposal to store location updates directly in PostgreSQL creates a conflict: (1) PostgreSQL is not designed for the write frequency required (potentially thousands of couriers updating every 1-5 seconds), (2) polling PostgreSQL for real-time reads introduces latency that eats into the 30-second window, (3) PostgreSQL has no native TTL mechanism to auto-enforce staleness — enforcement would be purely application-level with no infrastructure backstop, (4) coupling the real-time read path to PostgreSQL means a slow query or DB load spike directly degrades the customer experience. A pure PostgreSQL approach can technically implement the rule but cannot robustly guarantee it under load, which conflicts with Bird's 'must not be displayed' mandate. The cleanest architecture introduces a real-time cache layer (Redis with TTL), but this contradicts the team's stated approach.","routed_to":"Bird","options":["Option A: PostgreSQL-only — simpler infrastructure, but staleness enforcement is application-only (no infrastructure guarantee), write throughput is limited, and real-time push to customers requires polling or LISTEN/NOTIFY with limited fan-out. Risk: under load, the 30-second rule becomes a best-effort guideline rather than a hard invariant.","Option B: Redis cache for real-time reads + PostgreSQL for history — adds an infrastructure component but enforces the 30-second rule at the infrastructure level via TTL, supports high write throughput, and enables true real-time push. The staleness invariant becomes physically impossible to violate.","Option C: PostgreSQL with materialized view or logical replication to a cache — compromise that keeps PostgreSQL as primary but adds a derived real-time layer. More complex than Option B with fewer benefits."],"recommendation":"Option B. Bird's rule uses 'must not' — this is a hard invariant, not a soft goal. Hard invariants deserve infrastructure-level enforcement, not just application-level checks. Redis TTL makes stale data physically unreadable. Recommend Bird confirms the invariant is indeed hard (no grace period, no degraded mode) and Coach K approves the additional infrastructure component."}],"confidence":{"level":55,"high_confidence_areas":["The 30-second staleness rule is a hard invariant requiring infrastructure-level enforcement","PostgreSQL is the wrong tool for serving real-time location reads to customers","CQRS separation of real-time reads from historical persistence is the correct structural pattern","Domain events for async persistence decoupling"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the team has infrastructure budget/capability for Redis (could change the recommendation)","Exact courier update frequency and fleet size (affects whether PostgreSQL write throughput is actually a bottleneck)","Whether Bird's 'must not be displayed' allows a graceful degradation mode when all real-time infrastructure is down","Customer-facing protocol choice (WebSocket vs SSE vs polling) depends on client platform constraints not specified"],"assumptions":["Courier fleet is large enough (hundreds+) that write frequency matters","Customer expectation is sub-second location updates, not periodic refresh","The 30-second staleness rule has no exception or degradation mode","The team can provision and operate Redis or an equivalent in-memory store","Server-side timestamps are authoritative (courier device clocks are untrusted)"]}}
